title: PACER-Cert as a Benchmark for Stopping-Certificate Calibration in CPU-Only Active Causal Discovery
description: This project is positioned as an empirical benchmark and analysis paper, not a new active-discovery paradigm. It studies whether a lightweight edge-level stopping certificate for linear-Gaussian active causal discovery is actually calibrated, decision-useful, and trustworthy under strict CPU-only constraints.
motivation: AOED, Sample Efficient Bayesian Learning, and Bayesian Intervention Optimization already cover adaptive intervention design, finite-sample stopping, and decision-oriented scoring, so a broad novelty claim is not defensible. The narrower open question is whether a cheap, inspectable resolvability surrogate can support stopping decisions in a CPU-feasible discovery loop, and under what conditions that surrogate should be trusted.
proposed_approach: PACER-Cert maintains at most 24 weighted DAG particles initialized from bootstrap FGES CPDAGs and sampled DAG extensions. It scores candidate interventions by combining edge ambiguity, eliminable particle mass, and a local Gaussian KL-to-power surrogate, then stops when the remaining budget-resolvable ambiguity mass falls below a threshold. The main contribution is the evaluation protocol around that heuristic: 40-continuation calibration tests, oracle stopping-label prediction, a trust diagnostic based on disagreement with direct Monte Carlo lookahead, and a small ablation that replaces the surrogate with direct lookahead on tiny instances.

related_work.title: Adaptive Online Experimental Design for Causal Discovery
related_work.authors: Muhammad Qasim Elahi, Lai Wei, Murat Kocaoglu, Mahsa Ghasemi
related_work.how_it_differs: AOED is the mandatory stopping baseline because it already combines adaptive intervention design with an online termination rule. PACER-Cert differs mainly by using a capped particle set and an explicit edge-level resolvability certificate designed for CPU-feasible calibration analysis.

related_work.title: Sample Efficient Bayesian Learning of Causal Graphs from Interventions
related_work.authors: Zihan Zhou, Muhammad Qasim Elahi, Murat Kocaoglu
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper is the strongest novelty blocker on finite-sample active discovery with stopping. The present work therefore avoids any first-method claim and instead asks whether a much cheaper surrogate certificate can be calibrated well enough to be useful in practice.

related_work.title: Bayesian Intervention Optimization for Causal Discovery
related_work.authors: Yuxuan Wang, Mingzhou Liu, Xinwei Sun, Wei Wang, Yizhou Wang
related_work.how_it_differs: Bayesian Intervention Optimization already provides decision-oriented intervention scoring under uncertainty. PACER-Cert differs by focusing on edge-level predicted resolvability and stopping, using a local surrogate instead of global Bayesian optimization.

related_work.title: Trust Your ∇: Gradient-based Intervention Targeting for Causal Discovery
related_work.authors: Mateusz Olko, Michał Zając, Aleksandra Nowak, Nino Scherrer, Yashas Annadani, Stefan Bauer, Łukasz Kuciński, Piotr Miłoś
related_work.how_it_differs: GIT is the closest lightweight empirical baseline. PACER-Cert adds explicit stopping logic and a calibration study around whether predicted detectability helps beyond simple ambiguity or gradient sensitivity.

related_work.title: Power analysis for causal discovery
related_work.authors: Erich Kummerfeld, Leland Williams, Sisi Ma
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper motivates the finite-sample detectability issue but does not provide a sequential stopping certificate or a benchmark for evaluating calibration of edge-level resolvability predictions.

hypothesis: In linear-Gaussian active causal discovery with single-node interventions and fixed sample budgets, a lightweight edge-level resolvability surrogate can produce stopping signals that are better calibrated and more decision-useful than ambiguity-only heuristics, while remaining competitive with lightweight active baselines under CPU-only constraints.
success_criteria: The stopping score q_e(B_rem) achieves AUROC above 0.7 and better Brier score than PACER-no-D for oracle edge-resolvability labels derived from 40 continuation simulations.
The surrogate D(e | I,m) shows positive rank correlation and useful calibration against empirical resolvability across held-out decision states sampled from multiple policies.

On a tiny direct-lookahead ablation, PACER-Cert agrees with Monte Carlo lookahead on a clear majority of action rankings or stopping choices, showing that the certificate is informative rather than merely correlated with ambiguity.
PACER-Cert stops earlier than GIT and AOED-lite on weak-edge settings while losing at most 2 directed-edge-F1 points at the full budget, or improving on them.

The full study remains feasible on 2 CPU cores and within roughly 8 total hours by restricting the core benchmark to 24 synthetic instances and keeping direct Monte Carlo lookahead to a small diagnostic subset.
